chunk_text:
AHISA MEMBER SURVEY

The use of generative AI in Australian independent schools

As reported by AHISA members' schools July 2023

The use of generative AI in Australian independent schools

AHISA MEMBER SURVEY, JULY 2023

At the end of June 2023 AHISA launched a survey of members on the use of generative artificial intelligence (AI) tools in their schools. The survey also canvassed school leaders' opinions on and attitudes toward the potential impact of generative AI tools on school education, particularly teachers' work and student learning. Data gathered by the survey informed AHISA's submission to a parliamentary inquiry into the use of generative AI in the Australian education system. The release of ChatGPT in November 2022 and the rapidity and scale of its adoption have signalled that schools are on the cusp of profound technological disruption. As many AHISA members' schools were quick to investigate and experiment with AI tools, and as the impact of generative AI tools on school education is largely untested, the member survey offered the opportunity for AHISA to make a significant contribution to the inquiry. There were 130 responses to the survey, an exceptional response given the wide spread of dates for mid-year school holidays across the states and territories. This number represents 28 per cent of AHISA's membership and almost 11 per cent of all Australian independent schools. I am deeply grateful to AHISA members and to their staff members who may have assisted them to gather information for the survey. They have helped inform the understanding of other educators and of politicians and policy makers at what may prove to be a pivotal moment in the transformation of education delivery. I also gratefully acknowledge the contribution of education consultant Tom Barrett of Dialogic Learning to the development of the survey. Tom's close work with schools and educators and his early engagement with the use of generative AI tools in education proved invaluable. Dr Chris Duncan AHISA CEO 21 July 2023

heading_context: Positive impacts of generative AI observed in student work
chunk_text:
Policies & guidelines in schools

- 40% of respondents' schools have already covered the use of generative AI tools within existing policies addressing issues such as academic integrity, assessment or data and privacy
- 45% of respondents reported their school is in the process of developing a policy for staff
- 46% of respondents reported their school is in the process of developing a policy for students

Top 10 AI-assisted teacher tasks

1. Lesson plans or learning design
2. Learning resources
3. Ideas for curriculum unit outlines
4. Discussion questions
5. Rubrics for assessing student work
6. Questions for Q&A sessions
7. Summaries of articles
8. Student assessment tasks eg quizzes, essay topics
9. Articles for the school newsletter or school website
10. Differentiated learning tasks

|

Number of teachers using generative AI in respondents ' schools

- On average 24% and up to 72% of primary teachers
- On average 34.5% and up to 80% of middle school teachers
- On average 39% and up to 80% of secondary teachers

Top 3 benefits of generative AI tools for teachers ' work

1. Saves time
2. Helps to create a draft to get started
3. Supports the development of ideas

Top 3 challenges of using generative AI tools for teachers ' work

1. Lack of time to test the various applications with students
2. Learning to use the tools is too time consuming
3. Lack of school guidelines

Top 10 AI-assisted student tasks

1. Support student research
2. Generate ideas for creative projects
3. Offer feedback to improve written text
4. Draft or check coding
5. Find definitions of concepts that are more relevant or accessible
6. Check mathematical calculations
7. Generate presentation slides
8. Generate illustrations
9. Generate music
10. Generate animations

Positive impacts of generative AI observed in student work

- Improvements in drafting, creative inputs, brainstorming in creative work, generating ideas
- Assistance for students in research
- Improvements in the calibre of students' work. - Greater understanding of concepts
- Gains for students with literacy difficulties
- Improvement in student engagement

chunk_text:
1. PROFILE OF RESPONDENTS' SCHOOLS

There were 130 respondents to the survey, representing just over 28 per cent of AHISA members' schools. Most respondents' schools are in the most populous states.

Chart 1. Respondents' schools by state/territory

The respondents' schools were distributed across Australian states and territories as follows: New South Wales represented the largest share of respondents at 36%, followed by Victoria at 25% and Queensland at 20%. Western Australia accounted for 9%, South Australia for 5%, while the Australian Capital Territory and Tasmania each represented 2%. The Northern Territory accounted for 0%. - QLD: 20%
- NSW: 36%
- ACT: 2%
- VIC: 25%
- TAS: 2%
- NT: 0%
- SA: 5%
- WA: 9%

Most respondents' schools are located in major cities.

chunk_text:
Chart 2. School location according to the Australian Standard Geographic Classification (ASGC) system

Most respondent schools were located in major cities, representing 74% of the sample. Inner regional schools accounted for 15%, while outer regional schools represented 10%. Remote schools accounted for 1%, and very remote schools accounted for 0%. This indicates that the sample is strongly weighted toward urban and metropolitan school contexts. - Major City: 74%
- Inner Regional: 15%
- Outer Regional: 10%
- Remote: 1%
- Very Remote: 0%

Some 80% of respondents' schools have enrolments of 750 students and over. As respondents to the survey were self-selecting, this may indicate that larger schools have had greater workforce capacity to engage with generative AI tools at scale.

2. DEVELOPMENT OF POLICIES & GUIDELINES

as academic integrity, assessment or data and privacy and 5% reported their school had developed a stand-alone policy. A further 45% reported their school is in the process of developing a policy for staff and 46% reported they are in the process of developing a policy for students. Table 1. Current status of the development of school guidelines and policies relating to the use of generative AI tools

| Guidelines | |
|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----|
| We have established guidelines for teaching and education support staff use of generative AI tools | 19% |
| We are in the process of establishing guidelines for students' use of generative AI tools | 35% |
| We have established guidelines for students' use of generative AI tools | 17% |
| We have adopted or have drawn on existing guidelines for the use of generative AI, eg guidelines issued by education authorities in our state or territory, the European Commission's ethical guidelines for educators on the use of AI and data in education or England's Department for Education position statement on generative AI in education | 10% |
| Policies | |
| Use of generative AI has been added to existing policies covering issues such as academic integrity, assessment or data and privacy | 40% |
| Our generative AI policy is a stand-alone policy | 5% |
| We are in the process of developing a policy regarding the use of generative AI tools by teaching staff | 45% |
| We are in the process of developing a policy regarding the use of generative AI tools by administrative staff | 29% |
| We are in the process of developing a policy regarding the use of generative AI tools by students | 46% |
| We have not yet instituted a formal policy regarding the use of generative AI tools in our school, either as a stand-alone policy or referenced within existing policies | 43% |

In developing guidelines and policies, schools have referenced or are referencing documents from a range of organisations or entities, including their state and territory government education departments or education authorities.

chunk_text:
2. DEVELOPMENT OF POLICIES & GUIDELINES

of generative AI tools in our school, either as a stand-alone policy or referenced within existing policies | 43% | In developing guidelines and policies, schools have referenced or are referencing documents from a range of organisations or entities, including their state and territory government education departments or education authorities. International resources may also be referenced, including documents from the International Baccalaureate Organisation, OECD AI Policy Observatory, the European Commission and England's Department for Education. Some respondents commented on how their schools are experimenting with AI technology. One respondent reported their school is 'actively encouraging innovative use cases and individual exploration of AI in all contexts, without policy frameworks'. Another reported that while their school does not have a formal policy on the use of generative AI tools, staff and students are encouraged to look for opportunities for how the technology might be used across the school. Similarly, another respondent reported their school is still trialling different generative AI apps to gain an understanding of their potential and/or limitations. Schools are not necessarily working alone. One respondent commented: 'We are also working with a local team of educators to develop a regional, cross-sector response. There are working parties developing resources for different aspects of AI in education.' Another commented, 'We are developing university partnerships to support school-based action research in the use of AI for educational purposes'. As will become evident in later sections of this report, schools would welcome government guidelines, frameworks and policy development at system level, including regulation of generative AI companies in relation to data privacy and security.

heading_context: 3c. Benefits of generative AI tools for teachers' work
chunk_text:
3c. Benefits of generative AI tools for teachers' work

Respondents were asked to select from a list those benefits teachers commonly report when using generative AI tools for administrative tasks or assisting in teaching preparation tasks. Table 3. Benefits of generative AI tools to assist with teachers' work, ranked in order of the number of mentions, expressed as a proportion of respondents

| Saves time | 91% |
|----------------------------------------|-------|
| Helps to create a draft to get started | 78% |
| Supports the development of ideas | 70% |
| Provides new perspectives | 55% |
| Enhances creativity | 36% |
| Improves accuracy and consistency | 32% |
| Provides instant feedback | 31% |

Other benefits mentioned included:

- Wording and phrasing of information
- Refining already drafted communications
- Help with workload. Respondents also offered more generalised comment, including:

- 'Allows teachers to develop basic resources for students (like glossaries, background information, summarising information) quickly and efficiently. This allows teachers to spend more time where their individualised expertise is more valuable in giving feedback to students.'

- 'Allows for divergent thinking without becoming personal. Allows teachers to run thought experiments either individually or in teams and critique the outcomes. Different perspectives and ideas to those that would otherwise have been generated. Testing and checking of resources and thinking. Similar to what has been done through teacher associations and informal networks but can be done instantly, and on many devices, at a moment's notice.'

Not all respondents noted benefits from the use of generative AI tools: one respondent reported none of the benefits listed in the survey question were manifest.

heading_context: 3d. Challenges of using generative AI tools for teachers' work
chunk_text:
3d. Challenges of using generative AI tools for teachers' work

Respondents were asked to identify challenges teachers commonly report in the use of generative AI tools for administrative tasks or assisting in teaching preparation tasks from the following list, now ordered by number of mentions. Table 4. Challenges commonly reported by teachers in the use of generative AI tools in their work, ranked in order of the number of mentions, expressed as a proportion of respondents

| Lack of time to test the various applications with students | 50% |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------|
| Learning to use the tools is too time consuming | 41% |
| Lack of school guidelines | 39% |
| Lack of government guidelines | 27% |
| Lack of online video explainers or professional learning courses specifically targeting school teachers | 26% |
| Lack of interest and therefore support from other staff | 16% |
| School IT staff are not able to make the connection with pedagogy | 12% |

A range of other challenges was noted by respondents. There were several mentions of challenges relating to teachers' current lack of expertise and knowledge about generative AI tools, weaknesses in the tools themselves or potential issues arising from their use. The need for professional development and support materials was also noted:

- 'Lack of quality professional learning, with real world examples of how this can be used with students and by staff.'
- 'A challenge for non-users is time to explore the functionality of generative AI tools. More professional development and support materials are also required.'
- 'Lack of time to trial use of the multitude of tools that are available.'
- 'Lack of knowledge of how AI could be used to support their work.'
- 'Teachers' uncertainty about use of AI parameters.'
- 'Uncertainty about using AI without breaching copyright, assessment protocols etc.'
- 'Concern about authorship of generated works in relation to student work. Lack of maturity/nuance of generated works.

heading_context: 3d. Challenges of using generative AI tools for teachers' work
chunk_text:
3d. Challenges of using generative AI tools for teachers' work

'Lack of knowledge of how AI could be used to support their work.' - 'Teachers' uncertainty about use of AI parameters.' - 'Uncertainty about using AI without breaching copyright, assessment protocols etc.' - 'Concern about authorship of generated works in relation to student work. Lack of maturity/nuance of generated works. Ethical, IP issues.'

- 'Academic integrity concerns.'
- 'Issues of inaccuracy.'
- 'Inaccuracy of information diminishing confidence.'
- 'Potential lack of accuracy and validity of generated information.'
- 'Lack of specific professional learning developed for the use of AI or clear guidelines for teachers regarding AI limits the willingness of some teachers to experiment.'
- 'The speed with which new products are added to the market makes it difficult to keep up to date.'
- 'The widening gap between student skills/strengths and staff skills/strengths. Some have fears about ethics or appropriate use.'

Some respondents commented on teachers' attitudes, one noting that a challenge for teachers in the use of generative AI tools was 'getting over their sense of pride (in their minds, taking short cuts)'. Another commented, 'I think some teachers are fearful of using AI and worried about issues; others lack an appropriate level of caution'. One respondent wrote of generative AI as a challenge to the purpose of schooling:

'It breaches the purpose of schooling. School is a meaning making exercise and students and teachers must be viewed as meaning makers. It breaches community values because it prioritises education as status and output rather than meaning making. It breaches education as an act of love, because it helps to create human beings as autonomous, buffered selves. Nevertheless, once the value of the human being is unequivocally established, it can be an aid.'

heading_context: 3e. Generative AI tools most commonly used by teachers in their work
chunk_text:
3e. Generative AI tools most commonly used by teachers in their work

Respondents were asked to record, to their best knowledge, the three generative AI tools most commonly used by teachers in their school to assist in administrative and teaching preparation tasks. While a range of products were mentioned, ChatGPT received by far the most mentions - 55% of total product mentions. The next most mentioned were Microsoft Bing Chat (6% of total mentions) and Google Bard (6%), followed by Grammarly (4%) and text-to-image creators DALL-E (an OpenAI stablemate to ChatGPT) and Midjourney, both scoring 3% of all mentions. Other products mentioned were AskYourPDF, AIPRM (a prompt management tool and prompt library), Alli AI (search engine optimisation), Canva, DeepAI (text generator), Gamma (presentation tool), GitHub Copilot (text to code), Jasper (marketing tool), Learnt.ai, LessonLab, LinkReader, Magic School, Microsoft 365 Copilot, Nolej, Otter.ai (voice meeting notes and transcription), Perplexity, Photoshop, QuillBot, Quiz Well, Research Rabbit, Studyable, Synthesia (video generator), Teachers Assistant AI, Teachology.ai, TinyWow and Tome.

Other products mentioned were AskYourPDF, AIPRM (a prompt management tool and prompt library), Alli AI (search engine optimisation), Canva, DeepAI (text generator), Gamma (presentation tool), GitHub Copilot (text to code), Jasper (marketing tool), Learnt.ai, LessonLab, LinkReader, Magic School, Microsoft 365 Copilot, Nolej, Otter.ai (voice meeting notes and transcription), Perplexity, Photoshop, QuillBot, Quiz Well, Research Rabbit, Studyable, Synthesia (video generator), Teachers Assistant AI, Teachology.ai, TinyWow and Tome.

heading_context: Chart 7. Oversight of use of generative AI tools
chunk_text:
3f. Oversight and support for teachers ' and stud ents ' use of generative AI tools

Respondents were asked if their school had assigned a staff member to oversee the use of generative AI tools by teachers and/or students and invited to select a response from a list. |

Chart 7. Oversight of use of generative AI tools

Most schools reported that responsibility for overseeing generative AI tools was absorbed within an existing role or roles, representing 70% of responses. A further 14% reported that they had not yet appointed someone but were considering doing so. Informal interim arrangements were reported by 9%, while 7% selected another arrangement. This suggests that, in most schools, generative AI oversight has been added to existing responsibilities rather than formalised as a dedicated role. - This responsibility is absorbed within an existing role or roles: 70%
- Not yet, but we are considering this appointment: 14%
- We have an informal interim arrangement: 9%
- Other: 7%

The great majority of respondents reported their school had or was in the process of assigning oversight of the use of generative AI tools to a staff member. Respondents selecting 'other' reported their school has established a dual responsibility (Director of Teaching and Learning and Director of Digital Pedagogies), or had formed a committee or working group tasked with development and oversight of the school's approach to the use of generative AI tools. Among those selecting 'other' were those who reported either no assignment of the role had been made or had not yet been considered. |

heading_context: Chart 8. Students' permission to use generative AI tools in school
chunk_text:
4a. Permission to use generative AI tools in school

Survey respondents were asked to select from a list of options the approach that most generally applied to students' use of generative AI tools while at school. Respondents also chose 'other' to give further detail to their response.

Chart 8. Students' permission to use generative AI tools in school

Schools reported varied approaches to student use of generative AI tools. The largest share, 36%, allowed generative AI tools in certain classes for selected year levels and subjects. A further 26% allowed use in certain classes for selected year levels. However, 23% reported that students were not permitted to access any generative AI tools at school. Only 12% allowed access to school-approved generative AI tools, while 5% selected none of the listed options and 21% selected other arrangements. This indicates a mixed and still-developing approach to student access. - Students are not permitted to access any generative AI tools when at school: 23%
- Only school-approved generative AI tools are accessible to students when at school: 12%
- Generative AI tools are used in certain classes for selected year levels: 26%
- Generative AI tools are used in certain classes for selected year levels and subjects: 36%
- None of the above: 5%
- Other: 21%

Responses to the survey question reveal that student use of generative AI tools while at school is sanctioned in most respondents' schools. (As AHISA members self-selected to respond to the survey, this should not be interpreted as reflective of the situation in all AHISA members' schools or in all independent schools.)

Just over one-fifth of respondents reported that students are not permitted to access any generative AI tools when at school, although some commented that this was temporary to allow for guidelines to be established. The responses chosen and comments offered by respondents selecting 'other' indicate that the majority of schools - wherever they are on their digital journey - are being careful in how they introduce the use of generative AI tools to the classroom, taking into account a range of factors, including school guidelines and policies, teacher expertise, student readiness or the learning task, the home environment and privacy, safety and ethical concerns.

heading_context: Chart 9. Students' application of generative AI tools by order of number of mentions, expressed as a proportion of respondents selecting the item
chunk_text:
4b. Ways generative AI tools are used by students

Respondents were asked to select all items from a list of ways students might use generative AI tools that applied to students in their school.

Chart 9. Students' application of generative AI tools by order of number of mentions, expressed as a proportion of respondents selecting the item

Students' use of generative AI tools was most commonly reported for supporting student research, selected by 77% of respondents. Generating ideas for creative projects was selected by 68%, while offering feedback to improve written text was selected by 58%. Drafting or checking coding and finding more accessible or relevant definitions of concepts were each selected by 45%. Other uses included checking mathematical calculations at 32%, generating presentation slides at 31%, generating illustrations at 28%, generating music at 14%, generating animation at 8%, generating audio or voice tracks at 7%, and generating video at 6%. Five percent selected none of the above, and 13% selected other uses. - Support student research: 77%
- Generate ideas for creative projects: 68%
- Offer feedback to improve written text: 58%
- Draft or check coding: 45%
- Find definitions of concepts that are more relevant or accessible: 45%
- Check mathematical calculations: 32%
- Generate presentation slides: 31%
- Generate illustrations: 28%
- Generate music: 14%
- Generate animation: 8%
- Generate audio or voice tracks: 7%
- Generate video: 6%
- None of the above: 5%
- Other: 13%

'Other' applications mentioned by respondents include:

- Generating topic glossaries and revision/practice questions. - Developing contributions to school newsletters
- Summarising large text passages. One respondent commented: 'At this stage it is more a case of our students learning about AI rather than learning how best to use it, that is, learning about the tools and where AI touches our lives. The teacher demonstrates and leads/facilitates critical interrogation of the benefits and limitations of using the tools. Ethical dilemmas are also posed and responded to in the context of AI.'

|

4b. Ways generative AI tools are used by students

Respondents were asked to select all items from a list of ways students might use generative AI tools that applied to students in their school.

Chart 9. Students' application of generative AI tools by order of number of mentions, expressed as a proportion of respondents selecting the item

Students' use of generative AI tools was most commonly reported for supporting student research, selected by 77% of respondents. Generating ideas for creative projects was selected by 68%, while offering feedback to improve written text was selected by 58%. Drafting or checking coding and finding more accessible or relevant definitions of concepts were each selected by 45%. Other uses included checking mathematical calculations at 32%, generating presentation slides at 31%, generating illustrations at 28%, generating music at 14%, generating animation at 8%, generating audio or voice tracks at 7%, and generating video at 6%. heading_context: 4c. Improvements in student engagement or learning outcomes resulting from the use of generative AI tools in student work
chunk_text:
4c. Improvements in student engagement or learning outcomes resulting from the use of generative AI tools in student work

Respondents were asked to comment on whether improvements in student engagement or learning outcomes had resulted from the use of generative AI tools in student work. Only 84 of the overall 130 respondents completed the question. Of these respondents, 43% identified positive gains in either student engagement or learning outcomes or both. Some 45% commented that it was too early to ascertain any impact from the use of generative AI tools or that they had not yet developed ways to measure this, although some comments indicated schools were in the process of developing evaluative tools. Some 12% of those responding commented 'no' or 'none' without explanation, or mentioned negative outcomes. Those who specified negative outcomes most commonly mentioned issues with plagiarism. One respondent mentioned that minimal positive impacts had been observed and impacts were mostly negative. Another commented that no positive learning or engagement outcomes had been observed but that the use of generative AI tools had saved students time. One respondent commented, 'General consensus is that it is having a negative impact'. Another commented that, while no positive learning or engagement outcomes had yet been observed, students were interested and curious to know more. A range of positive impacts from the use of generative AI tools were noted by respondents, including:

- Improvements in drafting, creative inputs, brainstorming in creative work, generating ideas . One respondent commented, 'Students who use it as a creative or analytical tool benefit greatly'. - Assistance for students in research , including access to information and generating concise summaries of the information they find. One respondent commented, 'Students tend to be using the tools to check their understanding and also as a starting point for research and responses to questions'. - Improvements in the calibre of students' work . On respondent commented that 'the calibre of research is better after teaching students how to effectively use ChatGTP4 and we've seen the quality of their 'own' work improve'.

heading_context: 4c. Improvements in student engagement or learning outcomes resulting from the use of generative AI tools in student work
chunk_text:
4c. Improvements in student engagement or learning outcomes resulting from the use of generative AI tools in student work

understanding and also as a starting point for research and responses to questions'. - Improvements in the calibre of students' work . On respondent commented that 'the calibre of research is better after teaching students how to effectively use ChatGTP4 and we've seen the quality of their 'own' work improve'. Another respondent commented, 'Students are appreciative of teachers who scaffold its use and can collaboratively define with them what effective (and ethical) use could look like for a specific task'. Another respondent noted that 'Some students are proactively using AI platforms to refine and improve their work', while another added a caution: 'Students seem to be able to get fast and targeted feedback to support improvement in their work although it is still very early to tell if this is translating into increased understanding.'
- Greater understanding of concepts. One respondent commented that 'students using AI are demonstrating a greater level of knowledge of concepts'. - Gains for students with literacy difficulties , including an increase in their work output. One respondent commented, 'It has enabled students with low levels of literacy to contribute in meaningful ways and naturally boost their confidence'. - Improvement in student engagement . One respondent commented, 'Students are curious about these tools and will engage when they know they are being used in the learning process'. Another commented, 'Students have been engaged in learning more often and are able to produce better specific work'. Respondents also noted gains in student autonomy, increased capacity for learning and producing work and the benefit of scaffolded support for student learning and growth. One respondent noted a benefit to students from the use of generative AI tools as gaining 21st century skills and keeping up to date with changes in society norms.

chunk_text:
5a. School leaders' attitudes & opinions

Survey respondents were asked to indicate their level of agreement with the following comments on a scale of 1 to 5, from 'totally disagree', to 'totally agree'. As can be seen in the table below, school leaders express most agreement with the statements:

- 'I'm excited by the potential gains for student learning through generative AI tool.'
- 'Generative AI will not go away. Educators must familiarise themselves with the potential benefits and the potential harms as quickly as possible.'
- 'How student work and student learning are assessed will have to be rethought.'

The statement, 'Generative AI will tie up teachers' time in checking for plagiarism' received the lowest level of agreement. While school leaders are generally very positive about the potential gains from the use of generative AI tools in school education, there is also general agreement that 'we need to proceed with caution'. Leaders are also agreed on the need for 'clear system guidelines and regulations' to support the use of generative AI tools in schools. Table 5. School leaders' attitudes & opinions on aspects of the use of generative AI tools in school education

| | Totally disagree | Mostly disagree | Neither agree nor disagree | Generally agree | Totally agree |
|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|-------------------|------------------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Overall, generative AI is likely to make a positive contribution to student learning | 0% | 3% | 17% | 60% | 20% |
| We need to proceed with caution | 0% | 8% | 13% | 47% | 32% |
| I'm excited by the potential gains for student learning through generative AI tool | 0% | 2% | 8% | 54% | 37% |
| Generative AI will not go away.

source_file: markdown/cleaned/policy/australia/AHISA MEMBER SURVEY REPORT Gen AI in Australian independent schools.md

heading_context: Chart 10. School leaders' main hopes for generative AI gains in schools by order of number of mentions, expressed as a proportion of respondents selecting the item
chunk_text:
5b . School leaders' hopes for the application of generative AI tools in schools

Survey respondents were asked to choose from a list of statements any that reflected their main hopes for the positive impact of generative AI on schools. Reponses set out in the chart below indicate that many school leaders see the use of generative AI tools as providing benefits for both students and teachers and as linked to schools' preparation of students as 'future fit'.

Chart 10. School leaders' main hopes for generative AI gains in schools by order of number of mentions, expressed as a proportion of respondents selecting the item

School leaders' main hopes for generative AI focused on preparing students for the future of work, selected by 77% of respondents. Opportunities to personalise learning were selected by 76%, while automating tasks to free teachers' time and providing students with real-time feedback and support were each selected by 75%. Creating more immersive and interactive learning experiences was selected by 70%. New and innovative ways to connect with students was selected by 54%. Overall, the responses show strong expectations that generative AI may support future skills, personalised learning, teacher workload reduction, and student feedback. - Help prepare students for the future of work, which will increasingly require skills in creativity, problem solving, and critical thinking: 77%
- Opportunities to personalise learning for students: 76%
- Automate tasks, freeing up teachers' time to focus on more relational, creative and strategic work: 75%
- Provide students with real-time feedback and support: 75%
- Create more immersive and interactive learning experiences: 70%
- New and innovative ways to connect with students: 54%

Some respondents offered further comment. One respondent hopes that generative AI will 'force a redesign of assessment paradigms, force a rethink on the role of memory and test-taking abilities as proxies for intelligence, force a rethink of the role of a teacher in a classroom and force a rethink of the structures that underpin school operations'. Another expressed the hope that generative AI will 'provide students with assistance outside the classroom, thus reducing stress and anxiousness'. One respondent sees a role for generative AI as 'intelligent tutoring': 'AI-powered tutoring systems can provide real-time feedback and guidance to students, acting as virtual tutors. These systems can identify areas where students are struggling and offer targeted assistance, fostering independent learning and improvement.' Another emphasised the potential of generative AI to enable the 'development of unique, personalised and differentiated learning tasks, specific to the needs of the individual'. One respondent referred to generative AI's potential to improve accessibility and inclusion: 'AI can play a significant role in creating accessible learning environments for students with disabilities.

heading_context: Chart 10. School leaders' main hopes for generative AI gains in schools by order of number of mentions, expressed as a proportion of respondents selecting the item
chunk_text:
Chart 10. School leaders' main hopes for generative AI gains in schools by order of number of mentions, expressed as a proportion of respondents selecting the item

the potential of generative AI to enable the 'development of unique, personalised and differentiated learning tasks, specific to the needs of the individual'. One respondent referred to generative AI's potential to improve accessibility and inclusion: 'AI can play a significant role in creating accessible learning environments for students with disabilities. By providing real-time text-to-speech conversion, captioning and other assistive technologies, AI can ensure equal access to educational resources.'

|

Another respondent sees the potential of generative AI to improve schools' capacity for data analysis and predictive analytics: 'AI algorithms can analyse vast amounts of educational data, including student performance, attendance records, and engagement metrics. This information can be used to identify patterns, predict outcomes, and enable data-driven decision-making to enhance teaching and learning strategies.'

One respondent expressed a hope that generative AI can assist with 'hack work'. Another sees how generative AI will 'assist in explaining tasks in a different language automatically', although expresses concern for accuracy. Cautions were also sounded by other respondents. 'Do not overuse,' commented one respondent, while another hopes for the 'development of critical and ethical engagement with the technologies'. One respondent commented: 'I've ticked the box re future of work, but do not agree that AI is the best pathway for encouraging creativity, problem solving and critical thinking. These are the human elements of learning that need human nurturing.'

heading_context: 5c. School leaders' concerns about generative AI
chunk_text:
5c. School leaders' concerns about generative AI

Survey respondents were asked to record the extent of their concern about a range of issues that have been raised in relation to generative AI, rating their response on a scale from 'not at all concerned' to 'deeply concerned'. Table 6. School leaders' concerns about generative AI issues

| | Not at all concerned | Confident that concerns can be addressed | Somewhat concerned | Deeply concerned |
|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------|--------------------------------------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| Ethical issues such as inherent bias, intellectual property rights and data privacy | 3% | 14% | 48% | 35% |
| Detection and management of erroneous output from generative AI tools | 0% | 23% | 56% | 21% |
| Data security and privacy issues | 1% | 23% | 49% | 27% |
| Potential for the digital divide and equity gaps to widen | 10% | 23% | 44% | 23% |
| Decline in students' academic integrity | 5% | 41% | 35% | 19% |
| Decline in students' creativity | 22% | 39% | 27% | 12% |
| Decline in students' capacity to think deeply | 24% | 31% | 29% | 16% |
| The impact of social media issues on students will become more complex and more difficult to address | 4% | 29% | 50% | 16% |
| The impact of an ever-increasing pace of technological change on teacher wellbeing | 3% | 22% | 54% | 21% |
| The challenge of building capacity in the teacher workforce to harness the potential of generative AI | 0% | 24% | 58% | 18% |

|

Responses to these statements indicate that school leaders appear to have greater confidence in their opportunity to influence or manage issues relating to students' learning at school such as students' academic integrity, creativity and capacity to think deeply. School leaders' major concerns lie with generative AI tools themselves and wider system issues such as equity gaps in relation to students as well as teacher workforce issues.

heading_context: 5c. School leaders' concerns about generative AI
chunk_text:
5c. School leaders' concerns about generative AI

to influence or manage issues relating to students' learning at school such as students' academic integrity, creativity and capacity to think deeply. School leaders' major concerns lie with generative AI tools themselves and wider system issues such as equity gaps in relation to students as well as teacher workforce issues. They see an important role for government involvement at this level, but also want the autonomy to wield their educational expertise:

- 'This is an issue for schools that is emerging at a rapid speed. It offers a myriad of positive benefits but also presents an ever-increasing range of complex issues that schools and teachers must deal with. There needs to be consistency and clarity from government and educational leaders to support schools in their decision making. We cannot be in a situation where different schools make different decisions.'
- 'We do not need political leaders to make this area top heavy with burdensome regulation. We need political leaders to take the ethical heat of this new wave of change by challenging big companies to act ethically themselves. Teachers and schools do not have time to address the huge issues (both positive and negative) and need to be able to work within their sphere of influence with the trust that we have young people's best interests at heart.'

Respondents' comments expose the complexity of the issues involved around the use of generative AI in schools. Some raised the issue of equity gaps widening without strong policy parameters in place, although another respondent pointed out that 'AI can also be an equaliser, providing a level of support and feedback where formal tutoring is not an option'. It was also noted that, as well as potential equity gaps in students' access to generative AI tools, schools' lack of access to plagiarism checking software such as Turnitin when allowing students to use AI tools in their work would handicap those schools. It could also create grading inequities between students and across schools and undermine employer or public confidence in students' achievement.

heading_context: 5c. School leaders' concerns about generative AI
chunk_text:
5c. School leaders' concerns about generative AI

access to generative AI tools, schools' lack of access to plagiarism checking software such as Turnitin when allowing students to use AI tools in their work would handicap those schools. It could also create grading inequities between students and across schools and undermine employer or public confidence in students' achievement. Teachers' skills also represent an aspect of equity in education delivery, and one respondent noted a failure of regulatory bodies to ensure teachers' skills keep pace with technology and innovation. Another respondent wrote:

'Our teacher workforce is at a point where people are leaving if the learning demands are too high. It is really hard to strike a balance. Many people don't want to change what they do for assessment but just want a detection solution that will be absolute. I don't believe this is possible and I think assessment needs to reflect this and the world that young people will enter as adults. That requires considerable learning and a lot of reinvention and I think a lot of teachers are not prepared for this. We see teachers using AI to save time but some of that compromises student intellectual property. We have added a point to our teacher ICT policy that they cannot submit student work for feedback via AI. An irony of this concern is trusting Turnitin with the same content. However we have now also prevented one aspect of supporting a reduction in teacher workload with this point of protection for student IP.'

Another respondent commented: 'Students are too young to deal with the ethical and legal problems raised by the use of generative AI and, to remedy this, overburdened teachers now need to educate about this much more significantly than before even though many teachers lack the knowledge, skills and/or will to understand AI capabilities.'

The issue of upskilling teachers was raised by another respondent: 'We need to support teacher learning with regular and strategic points of entry to support the range of the readiness spectrum. We have done our best so far to adjust documentation and support teachers and students.

heading_context: 5c. School leaders' concerns about generative AI
chunk_text:
5c. School leaders' concerns about generative AI

to understand AI capabilities.' The issue of upskilling teachers was raised by another respondent: 'We need to support teacher learning with regular and strategic points of entry to support the range of the readiness spectrum. We have done our best so far to adjust documentation and support teachers and students. We have been working hard to keep a smaller group highly informed and to break down the firehose of information for teachers and direct them to the best resources.'

Other respondents warned against an over-emphasis on technology. One commented: 'The purpose of education will be distorted by the 'need' to respond to AI. It is important that students are able to harness this emergent technology. However, there are other important priorities of education that should not be compromised by focusing too much on technology in classrooms.'

Other concerns raised related to the potential impact of generative AI on the development of authentic student voice, and the need to address students' emotional intelligence. Mention was also made of the reality of generative AI embedded in digital products commonly used in schools. One respondent commented, 'As a Microsoft Showcase School, our extensive use of Office 365 will significantly evolve and be impacted by Microsoft's investment in AI technologies and integration within its core suite of applications used by staff and students'. One respondent sees a silver lining in the influence of 'edtech' in education: 'I fear an environment that seeks to control the impact of generative AI. It is akin to holding back the tide: you will not be able to use Microsoft Word in three months without it running in the background. This is the catalyst for us to reimagine assessment and (hopefully) see the death of memory and hot-housed recall as markers of educational success.'

chunk_text:
5d. Parental concerns

Survey respondents were invited to select from a list of possible concerns that may have been raised by parents about the use of generative AI tools in the education of their children. As the chart overleaf suggests, at this point parents are more likely to express appreciation for the potential of generation AI tools to support their children's learning than undermine it. As one respondent commented, 'Comments from parents have been minimal and more curious than concerned.'

Additional comments contributed by respondents identified parental concerns linked to assessment and academic integrity:

- 'The biggest concern we are facing from parents is to do with academic integrity and their children being unfairly punished for misusing AI tools when completing assessment tasks.'
- 'There are concerns around academic integrity and its detection.'
- 'Some are concerned about their child being disadvantaged if others use generative AI to cheat and do better in assessments.'
- 'Some have expressed concern about academic integrity, particularly in terms of impact on student achievement in assessment tasks not being valid or reliable.'

While one respondent commented that 'Schools need support on how to document this matter to parents', schools are also purposefully engaging with parents on the use of generative AI in education, as this comment attests: 'We have actively engaged parents in the AI conversation. They know we are feeling our way, and they have access to our best thinking on AI. It is the best that we can do. They accept its use in broader workplaces and environments, they are uncertain as to how it will impact them personally, but we are opening up our skill building activities to them, training them.' ◼
heading_context_chunk_text:
5d. Parental concerns

Survey respondents were invited to select from a list of possible concerns that may have been raised by parents about the use of generative AI tools in the education of their children. As the chart overleaf suggests, at this point parents are more likely to express appreciation for the potential of generation AI tools to support their children's learning than undermine it. As one respondent commented, 'Comments from parents have been minimal and more curious than concerned.'

Additional comments contributed by respondents identified parental concerns linked to assessment and academic integrity:

- 'The biggest concern we are facing from parents is to do with academic integrity and their children being unfairly punished for misusing AI tools when completing assessment tasks.'
- 'There are concerns around academic integrity and its detection.'
- 'Some are concerned about their child being disadvantaged if others use generative AI to cheat and do better in assessments.'
- 'Some have expressed concern about academic integrity, particularly in terms of impact on student achievement in assessment tasks not being valid or reliable.'

While one respondent commented that 'Schools need support on how to document this matter to parents', schools are also purposefully engaging with parents on the use of generative AI in education, as this comment attests: 'We have actively engaged parents in the AI conversation. They know we are feeling our way, and they have access to our best thinking on AI. It is the best that we can do. They accept its use in broader workplaces and environments, they are uncertain as to how it will impact them personally, but we are opening up our skill building activities to them, training them.' ◼

heading_context: Chart 11. Parents' concerns about generative AI, by order of number of mentions as expressed as a proportion of respondents selecting the item
chunk_text:
Chart 11. Parents' concerns about generative AI, by order of number of mentions as expressed as a proportion of respondents selecting the item

Most respondents reported that parents had not voiced concerns to the school about the use of AI tools, representing 65% of responses. Some respondents reported that parents had expressed appreciation for the ways AI tools might enhance learning and were supportive of integration, at 28%. Concerns were also reported: 23% indicated that some parents feared their children's learning may suffer if they are given access to AI tools, while 18% indicated concern that children may be disadvantaged if they do not have access to AI tools for learning activities. Smaller proportions reported concerns that AI may stifle children's creativity, at 9%, or compromise the quality of teachers' work, also at 9%. Concern that the quality of teachers' reports on children's progress may decline was reported by 1%. - Parents have not voiced any concerns to the school about the use of AI tools: 65%
- Some have expressed appreciation for the ways AI tools might enhance the learning experience and are supportive of integration: 28%
- Some parents fear that their children's learning may suffer if they are given access to AI tools: 23%
- Conversely, some worry that their children may be at a disadvantage if they do not have access to AI tools for learning activities: 18%
- There are some concerns that the use of AI tools may stifle their children's creativity: 9%
- Some worry that the quality of teachers' work may be compromised: 9%
- Some are concerned that the quality of teachers' reports on their children's progress may decline: 1%

chunk_text:
AI systems in teaching and learning: principles and practical examples, Building a PATH forward

Jonathan Brazil, Dr Suijing Yang, Dr Fabienne van der Kleij AI systems in teaching and learning: Principles and practical examples. Building a PATH forward Jonathan Brazil, Suijing Yang and Fabienne van der Kleij The Australian Council for Educational Research Ltd 19 Prospect Hill Road Camberwell VIC 3124 Phone: (03) 9277 5555

PATH principles for AI in education

This figure presents the PATH principles as a framework for the responsible use of AI in education. The acronym PATH is represented through four connected arrow-shaped blocks: P, A, T, and H. Each letter corresponds to one principle. - P — Promote teaching and learning: Use AI to enhance teaching and learning when the benefits outweigh the costs, evaluate AI use, and encourage effective thinking and assessment practices. - A — Advance wellbeing and social interaction: Use AI to support the wellbeing and social interaction of both educators and learners. - T — Train for AI literacy: Equip both educators and students with the knowledge and values to understand and use AI ethically and effectively. - H — Harness AI ethically: Align AI use with broad ethical principles of transparency, responsibility and accountability, privacy, justice and fairness, and human, child, and worker rights. Transforming learning with AI systems

The rapid popularisation of AI systems signifies a major shift in teaching and learning, bringing both new opportunities and significant challenges. Therefore, as education systems worldwide confront two key imperatives-preparing learners for an increasingly unpredictable and rapidly evolving future and ensuring every student is fully engaged, successful, and ready for the demands of tomorrow (ACER, 2022)-understanding how to maximise the benefits of AI systems while overcoming its challenges becomes a critical priority. To help guide educators, this report:

1. Outlines a set of guiding principles for using AI systems in teaching and learning. 2. Provides practical examples of how AI systems can be used in teaching and learning.

chunk_text:
What is an AI system?

There are numerous definitions of AI systems. Here, we use the following definition:

An AI system is a machine-based system that, for explicit or implicit objectives, infers, from the input it receives, how to generate outputs such as predictions, content, recommendations, or decisions that can influence physical or virtual environments. Different AI systems vary in their levels of autonomy and adaptiveness after deployment. (OECD, 2024, p. 4)

A generative AI system is a type of AI system that is designed to create new content or data, such as text, images, audio, video, code, or other formats, that resembles or mimics the patterns found in its training data. These systems rely on advanced machine learning models, often based on neural networks. Generative AI systems work by learning the underlying structure and features of their training data during the training phase. Once trained, they can produce original outputs that are not direct replicas of the input data but follow the same style, rules, or patterns. For instance:

- Text Generation . Creating coherent and contextually relevant writing (e.g., ChatGPT; openai.com). - Image Generation . Producing realistic or stylised images based on descriptions or prompts (e.g., MidJourney; midjourney.com). - Music Generation. Composing melodies or full tracks (e.g., Amper Music; ampermusic.com). - Code Generation. Writing or suggesting code snippets for developers (e.g., GitHub Copilot; github.com). - Video Generation. Creating or editing video content (e.g., Runway ML; runwayml.com). - Speech and Voice Generation. Synthesising realistic speech or creating custom voiceovers (e.g., Descript Overdub; descript.com).

heading_context: Opportunities and challenges of AI systems for teaching and learning
chunk_text:
Opportunities and challenges of AI systems for teaching and learning

AI systems have the potential to revolutionise education, transforming teaching and learning through innovative applications (Yan et al., 2024). For instance, by automating routine tasks like grading and attendance tracking, AI can enhance efficiency, freeing teachers to focus on more complex and creative aspects of education (Wang et al., 2024). It also may support personalised learning by providing tailored assessments and feedback, thereby improving learning effectiveness by addressing the unique needs of individual students (Wang et al., 2024). Beyond enhancing existing practices, AI enables the reimagining of learning experiences. Adaptive learning systems, for instance, have the potential to adjust to a learner's knowledge, motivation and emotions, creating a dynamic and responsive educational environment (Wang et al., 2024). Furthermore, AI opens doors to previously unfeasible opportunities, such as immersive VR and AR simulations that may provide learners access to experiences not previously possible (Wang et al., 2024). However, the integration of AI in teaching and learning also has its challenges (e.g., Adams et al., 2023). Concerns must be carefully addressed about impacts on teaching and learning, wellbeing and social interaction, AI literacy of users, transparency, responsibility and accountability, privacy, justice and fairness, and human, child, and workers' rights. By navigating these challenges thoughtfully, AI can become a transformative tool in fostering positive outcomes in education.

chunk_text:
Overview of the guidance

This document has two main sections. First, we present principles for using AI for teaching and learning. Second, we provide practical examples of how AI can be used for teaching and learning. To introduce the principles, a human-centred approach is put forward as an overarching principle for using AI in teaching and learning (similar to UNESCO, 2021). This is an approach where technology is designed and used in ways that prioritise human interests (Shneiderman, 2022). This human-centred approach is embodied in the PATH principles proposed in this document. These principles aim to help educators navigate the integration of AI into teaching and learning ethically and effectively. The PATH principles are:

1. Promote teaching and learning . Use AI to enhance teaching and learning when the benefits outweigh the costs, evaluate AI use, and encourage effective thinking and assessment practices. 2. Advance wellbeing and social interaction . Use AI to support the wellbeing and social interaction of both educators and learners. 3. Train for AI literacy . Equip both educators and students with the knowledge and values to understand and use AI ethically and effectively. 4. Harness AI Ethically . Align AI use with broad ethical principles of transparency, responsibility and accountability, privacy, justice and fairness, and human, child, and worker rights. Whilst generated in Australia, the guidance outlined in this document draws upon international evidence and is applicable in a range of international contexts and school settings. In interpreting and applying the contents of this document, readers are encouraged to do so within the relevant legislative and regulatory frameworks within their specific contexts.
heading_context_chunk_text:
Overview of the guidance

This document has two main sections. First, we present principles for using AI for teaching and learning. Second, we provide practical examples of how AI can be used for teaching and learning. To introduce the principles, a human-centred approach is put forward as an overarching principle for using AI in teaching and learning (similar to UNESCO, 2021). This is an approach where technology is designed and used in ways that prioritise human interests (Shneiderman, 2022). This human-centred approach is embodied in the PATH principles proposed in this document. These principles aim to help educators navigate the integration of AI into teaching and learning ethically and effectively. The PATH principles are:

1. Promote teaching and learning . Use AI to enhance teaching and learning when the benefits outweigh the costs, evaluate AI use, and encourage effective thinking and assessment practices. 2. Advance wellbeing and social interaction . Use AI to support the wellbeing and social interaction of both educators and learners. 3. Train for AI literacy . Equip both educators and students with the knowledge and values to understand and use AI ethically and effectively. 4. Harness AI Ethically . Align AI use with broad ethical principles of transparency, responsibility and accountability, privacy, justice and fairness, and human, child, and worker rights. Whilst generated in Australia, the guidance outlined in this document draws upon international evidence and is applicable in a range of international contexts and school settings. In interpreting and applying the contents of this document, readers are encouraged to do so within the relevant legislative and regulatory frameworks within their specific contexts.

chunk_text:
Development of the guidance

The aim of this research was to provide educational professionals with guidance on using AI effectively and ethically for teaching and learning. To this end, we posed the following two research questions:

1. What guiding principles should be considered when using AI for teaching and learning? 2. How can AI meaningfully be used in teaching and learning?

Development of the PATH principles

To answer the first research question, the initial strategy was to conduct a rapid review. During this process, existing reviews were explored, leading to the identification of a synthesis of AI principles in education as per Adams et al. (2023) (see Figure 1, step 1). Adams et al.'s principles extend the work of Jobin et al.'s (2019) review of 85 ethical frameworks for AI use by synthesising education-specific guidance from The World Economic Forum (2019), The Institute for Ethical AI in Education (IEAIE) (2021), UNESCO (2021), and UNICEF (2021). Although Adams et al.'s (2023) framework offers guidance for using AI in teaching and learning, it presents several limitations for broader application:

1. The framework focuses on K-12 education, potentially overlooking principles related to tertiary education. 2. The documents reviewed may not have specifically addressed the unique opportunities and challenges posed by generative AI. 3. The analysis does not incorporate more recent guidance, which could offer updated insights. 4. The principles lack coherence, as some principles overlap significantly. 5. The presentation and articulation of the principles could be refined to enhance their clarity and applicability for educators. Therefore, additional documents were analysed by the first author to evaluate how well Adams et al.'s framework aligns with more recent guidelines, as well as its coherence and usability (Figure 1, Step 2). This resulted in the development of a more comprehensive, up-to-date, coherent, and potentially more practical set of principles (see Figure 1, Step 3). Figure 1. Steps in the development of the PATH Principles for using AI in teaching and learning

heading_context: Development process for the PATH principles
chunk_text:
Development process for the PATH principles

This figure shows a three-step process used to develop the PATH principles. 1. Identification: An existing review of principles and more recent guidance is identified. 2. Analysis: The identified material is analysed in terms of alignment, coherence, and usability. 3. Development: The PATH principles are then developed from this analysis. To identify more recent guidance (Figure 1, Step 1), a literature search was conducted using both traditional academic databases and multinational institutional libraries, such as the OECD, as traditional academic database searches overlooked these materials (Adams et al., 2023). General web searches were also conducted to locate additional guidelines in line with Adams et al. (2023). This approach led to the identification of four high-quality sources not included in Adams et al.'s synthesis (see Table 1). These documents were used to provide further elaboration and broader evidence for the set of principles identified by Adams et al. (2023) for the use of AI in teaching and learning. Table 1. Additional guidance used to inform the PATH principles beyond those used by Adams et al. (2023)

| Title | Year | Creator and link |
|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Australian Framework for Generative Artificial Intelligence in Schools | 2023 | Commonwealth of Australia https://www.education.gov.au/schooling/resources/aus tralian-framework-generative-artificial-intelligence-ai- schools |
| AI Guidance for Schools Toolkit | 2023 | Code.org, Consortium for School Networking, Digital Promise, European EdTech Alliance, Larimore, J., & Policy Analysis for California Education https://www.teachai.org/toolkit-principles |
| Opportunities, Guidelines and Guardrails for Effective and Equitable Use of AI in Education | 2023 | OECD https://doi.org/10.1787/2b39e98b-en |
| Generative AI in Higher Education: A Global Perspective of Institutional Adoption Policies and Guidelines | 2024 | Jin, Y., Yan, L., Echeverria, V., Gašević, D.,& Martinez - Maldonado, R. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2405.11800 |

After reviewing the additional guidance, an analysis was conducted to evaluate its alignment with the principles established by Adams et al. (2023). Overall, there was substantial alignment between Adams et al.'s principles and the more recent guidance. However, the newer guidance offered further elaboration and introduced additional considerations. For example, the newer guidance led to:

- Expanded principles .

heading_context: Development process for the PATH principles
chunk_text:
Development process for the PATH principles

to evaluate its alignment with the principles established by Adams et al. (2023). Overall, there was substantial alignment between Adams et al.'s principles and the more recent guidance. However, the newer guidance offered further elaboration and introduced additional considerations. For example, the newer guidance led to: - Expanded principles . For instance, Adams et al.'s principle of 'Teacher wellbeing' was broadened to include the wellbeing of students and other stakeholders; the principle on 'Children's Rights' was extended to incorporate 'Human and Worker Rights'. - New additions and sub-components . For instance, 'Thinking' was added as a sub-component under 'Promote teaching and learning', and 'Academic integrity' was incorporated as a subcomponent of 'Transparency'. Further, the principle of 'Responsibility and accountability' was broken down into the sub-components of 'Oversight and human agency', 'Contestability and remedy', 'Protection, empowerment, and human Support', and 'Evaluation'. In considering the coherence and usability of Adams et al.'s principles for educators, it was concluded that improvements could be achieved by consolidating overlapping principles and grouping broadly applicable ethical principles under a single overarching principle-'Harness AI Ethically'-for greater clarity. This approach helped distinguish education-specific ethical principles from those applicable outside of education settings while also reducing the overall number of principles to a more manageable and memorable set. The principles of 'Beneficence,' 'Non-maleficence,' and 'Freedom and Autonomy' from Adams et al. were found to overlap with the principles of 'Advancing wellbeing and social interaction' and 'Responsibility and accountability' proposed here. Therefore, these principles were consolidated. Furthermore, to increase engagement and memorability of the principles for educational professionals, the acronym 'PATH' and infographic were created. Clearer and more actionable labels were also introduced to enhance usability. In other words, the principles presented in this document are consistent with those of Adams et al. (2023) but elaborated upon and presented in an arguably more coherent and useable manner for educators. Additionally, the alignment of recent guidelines with these principles ensures greater relevance.

heading_context: Figure 2. The PATH principles for using AI in teaching and learning
chunk_text:
Figure 2. The PATH principles for using AI in teaching and learning

This figure gives a clearer view of the four PATH principles for AI in education. The framework is organised around four connected principles represented by the letters P, A, T, and H. - Promote teaching and learning: AI should be used to enhance teaching and learning when benefits outweigh costs. Its use should be evaluated, and it should support effective thinking and assessment practices. - Advance wellbeing and social interaction: AI should support the wellbeing and social interaction of both educators and learners. - Train for AI literacy: Educators and students should be equipped with the knowledge and values needed to understand and use AI ethically and effectively. - Harness AI ethically: AI use should be aligned with ethical principles, including transparency, responsibility, accountability, privacy, justice, fairness, and human, child, and worker rights.

chunk_text:
1. Promote teaching and learning

Due to the affordances provided by AI systems, their use in teaching and learning should be considered. However, as the guidelines reviewed make clear, AI should not be used haphazardly. Instead of using AI due to hype, it is essential to ensure its use promotes teaching and learning, which has three essential components:

1. Benefit . The use of AI should be considered when the benefits outweigh the costs. 2. Evaluation . The evaluation of AI should be considered to ensure it effectively enhances the educational experience. 3. Thinking. To effectively encourage and evaluate learning, educators should consider designing work that AI cannot complete, encouraging higher-order thinking, creativity, and critical analysis of AI, and providing clear guidance regarding how students can use AI in their work, such as during assessments. As highlighted in the creation of many of the documents and in some of their guidance, co-creation should be considered when designing and evaluating AI. For instance, in consultation with various stakeholders during the creation of the guidelines reviewed, it was highlighted that while there is much excitement that AI systems may provide more access to personalised learning pathways tailored to each student's unique needs, there are concerns. One major challenge stakeholders discussed was the potential reduction in student-teacher relations (see IEAIED, 2021b), which are vital for learning and engagement (Roorda et al., 2011). Therefore, when deciding to implement an AI system that offers personalised learning pathways, educators should carefully evaluate if the benefit of the system outweighs any costs, such as loss of social interaction. Additionally, it is important to consider strategies for mitigating these costs, regularly assess the AI system's overall impact and fit, and consider co-creating AI solutions and evidence with numerous stakeholders. Another aspect of prioritising learning is to consider designing work for students that AI cannot complete and encouraging higher-order thinking, creativity, and critical thinking. For instance, educators can use oral presentations or invigilated assessments to assess students' ability to apply and evaluate information and think critically, ensuring the work reflects their own thinking, knowledge, and capabilities.
heading_context_chunk_text:
1. Promote teaching and learning

Due to the affordances provided by AI systems, their use in teaching and learning should be considered. However, as the guidelines reviewed make clear, AI should not be used haphazardly. Instead of using AI due to hype, it is essential to ensure its use promotes teaching and learning, which has three essential components:

1. Benefit . The use of AI should be considered when the benefits outweigh the costs. 2. Evaluation . The evaluation of AI should be considered to ensure it effectively enhances the educational experience. 3. Thinking. To effectively encourage and evaluate learning, educators should consider designing work that AI cannot complete, encouraging higher-order thinking, creativity, and critical analysis of AI, and providing clear guidance regarding how students can use AI in their work, such as during assessments. chunk_text:
1. Promote teaching and learning

to consider designing work for students that AI cannot complete and encouraging higher-order thinking, creativity, and critical thinking. For instance, educators can use oral presentations or invigilated assessments to assess students' ability to apply and evaluate information and think critically, ensuring the work reflects their own thinking, knowledge, and capabilities. Additionally, educators can use collaborative projects, where students work together to solve complex, open-ended problems that encourage innovation, teamwork, and adaptability. The second section of this report provides some practical examples of using of AI systems for teaching and learning. Some potential considerations when evaluating AI systems and their use are:

- Effectiveness . Does AI use achieve the intended goal? Are the benefits worth the costs? (i.e., Does the use of AI align with 'Principle 1. Promoting Teaching and Learning'?)
- Efficiency . Does AI use achieve the intended goal in a timely manner? - Wellbeing and social interaction. What is the impact of AI on student and/or teacher wellbeing and social interaction? (see 'Principle 2. Advance wellbeing and social interaction')
- AI literacy. Do users have appropriate levels of AI literacy, such as how to use the system effectively and ethically? (see 'Principle 3. Train for AI literacy')
- Ethics. Does the use of AI align with broad ethical principles for AI use, such as justice and fairness, privacy, and transparency? (see 'Principle 4. Harness AI ethically')

The Institute for Ethical AI in Education (2021a) has provided further evaluation criteria that can be used during pre-procurement, procurement, monitoring and evaluation, and implementation phases of AI systems. Code.org et al. (2023, pp. 16-20) lists further benefits, risks, and mitigation strategies when employing AI systems. Table 2 summarises how the reviewed documents address the themes of benefit, evaluation, and thinking in the context of promoting teaching and learning. Table 5 in the Appendix includes further details. Table 2.

chunk_text:
2. Advance wellbeing and social interaction

When using AI in education, it is important to prioritise the wellbeing of teachers, students, and other stakeholders and foster meaningful social interactions. At a minimum, it is important to consider if AI is safe and harm-free 1 . Moreover, AI use ideally attempts to enhance-rather than replace-human teaching and crucial human social connections, such as those between students and teachers or among students and their peers. The use of AI should be considered to reduce teachers' workloads and stressors, thereby increasing their capacity to focus on core teaching activities. Additionally, teachers' expertise should be considered, with AI used in a way that respects and augments their human decision-making. Teachers' rights and working conditions are important considerations as well. Student wellbeing is also paramount. AI tools should aim to support a balanced academic experience. Preserving students' digital and non-digital activities such as play and leisure time, social interactions, and school breaks is important. Rather than increasing pressure around homework and assessments, AI should be considered to help alleviate these stressors. Finally, AI systems focused on enhancing wellbeing should also be considered; for example, systems that can identify when students or teachers may require mental health support can assist in providing targeted support as required. This principle also intersects with other principles, such as AI literacy, as having knowledge of how to use AI can prevent unnecessary stress and workload for teachers as they navigate AI integration in their classrooms. Ensuring teachers are equipped with the necessary skills to evaluate AI tools fosters a balanced and informed approach to technology adoption in education. Elements of evaluation and co-design discussed in the previous section are relevant here as well; part of evaluating AI is to weigh up the benefits or costs associated with wellbeing . Including teachers and students in the design and feedback processes of AI tools is also helpful for ensuring that these tools align with advancing enduser wellbeing. Box 1 outlines how advancing wellbeing and social interaction are discussed in the reviewed guidance.

Including teachers and students in the design and feedback processes of AI tools is also helpful for ensuring that these tools align with advancing enduser wellbeing. heading_context: Box 1. References to advancing wellbeing and social interaction in the reviewed guidance
chunk_text:
Box 1. References to advancing wellbeing and social interaction in the reviewed guidance

| • | The Institute for Ethical AI in Education (2021a) emphasises wellbeing under the principle of 'Administration and Workload,' stating that 'AI should increase the capacity of organisations whilst respecting human relationships' (The Ethical Framework for AI in Education). |
|-----|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| • | UNESCO (2021) advocates to 'Ensure that AI is used to empower teachers' (p. 36) Their recommendations underscore protecting students' social interactions, play, leisure time, and school breaks. They highlight that AI tools should alleviate rather than intensify pressures related to homework and exams. Furthermore, the impact of AI on student wellbeing should be regularly assessed through co-creation and feedback processes, ensuring AI enhances rather than replaces essential teacher functions. |
| • | The World Economic Forum (2019), participants discussed that policy should 'prescribe steps to mitigate potential harms' (p. 15) |
| • | UNICEF (2021) include a recommended requirement to 'Support children's development and well-being' (p. 32). They recommended the following: 'Prioritize how AI systems can benefit children, particularly in AI policies and strategies,' 'Develop and apply a design for a child rights approach,' 'Leverage AI systems to support and increase environmental sustainability,' and 'Integrate metrics and processes to support children's well-being in the use of AI.' |
| • | The Commonwealth of Australia (2023) includes the principle of 'Human and Social Wellbeing,' asserting that 'Generative AI tools are used to benefit school community' (p. 6). |
| • | Code.org et al.'s (2023) 'Balance' principle mentions: 'While generative AI presents useful assistance to amplify teachers' capabilities and reduce teacher workload, these technologies should be a supporting tool to augment human judgment, not replace it' (p. 16). |
| • | OECD (2023) has a principle of 'Student and teacher wellbeing' (p. 9), stating that the use of AI-enabled technology should prioritise mental health and balance digital and non-digital activities. It emphasises that the social and relational experience of learning should be communicated, that AI systems have the potential to support wellbeing, and that it is important to foster AI literacy to ensure safe and conducive learning. |
